column9: With a peer, when working with a peer I feel like they understand the problem and the codebase better. With a peer you can often have random discussions that unexpectedly help solve an unrelated problem, which is something I have yet to see with AI. For repetitive tasks, like logging, using AI is often useful, because it reduces the amount of boilerplate code I have to write.
column10: It depends on the situation. If both me and my coworker are focused on the same problem, I tend to stay focused on the task. If the coworker messages or comes to see me for an unrelated task, I’m less focused, whereas Copilot will not distract me as often. I should not that sometimes copilot makes certain suggestions that aren’t very relevant to the problem at hand, and sometimes that makes me lose focus, because I try to understand why it would suggest something unrelated.
column11: I believe my code quality is better with a peer, since the peer will also focus on readability, and not just the output. Furthermore, the peer also has codebase specific knowledge, they can help reduce the quantity of code I rewrite, because I could reuse a function of class defined somewhere else in the codebase. This is something I noticed copilot struggle with if the function or class is not in the same file.
column12: It depends, for similar reasons stated in Question 15. I think I’m faster with Copilot when writing tests, since the logic for testing is often repetitive and I can very easily validate the output produced by Copilot to make sure the code does what it’s intended to do.
column13: When consulting a peer. Copilot can often hallucinate and produce code that seems correct. Consulting with a peer gives me more confidence that my code is correct.
column14: I retain information more easily when consulting a peer, since I’m actively focusing on the code and task at hand. With Copilot, it’s very easy to go on autopilot and lose focus, since I’m not as actively engaged as wen I’m coding on my own or with a peer. I also think discussing a problem with a peer helps you remember the problem and solution more easily, since you tend to remember discussions with people, whereas discussing with AI is often less memorable.

column9: I felt a better flow in communication when talking with a peer. While Copilot's answers were accurate and pretty easy to understand, it seemed like the peer wanted to truly help me and understand the code at the same time as me. I definitely preferred the latter.
column10: I did not feel like my concentration was better with one or another. However, I felt like I did not want to waste the peer's time or ask her too many questions. This made me more nervous and more inclined to really try and understand the questions by myself.
column11: I think that the quality of my code is better when working with a peer. GitHub Copilot did not seem to take into account many of the functions already implemented in the provided code. It might have been because of a mistake in my questions, but I feel like the peer was much more considerate of everything that is needed to answer the question and will "see" the project in a way similar of the one of the user (me).
column12: In this case, I feel like I work faster with GitHub Copilot. Since I was nervous trying to understand the question when working with a peer, my brain was more in a "panic" mode than a "comprehension" mode. Working with Copilot allowed me to take the time I needed to understand the question, since the AI will never have to wait for me or be overwhelmed by my questions.
